## [12:06 - 15:07]

So typically, Facebook takes a 3-2 approach. They look at the ad and they look at it for essentially, does the ad make sense? Does it have any unsubstantiated claims or sensational claims? Does it imply any negative attributes? Does it withhold information? And we'll go through each of these in a bit more detail. But I just kind of want to give you an overview of what they're looking at when they're looking at the ad. And lastly, does the ad accurately reflect the product sold on the landing page? And often there's a disconnect with claims and whether or not it's accurately reflecting. And they see that as withholding and clickbaity. They are really trying to move away from those types of advertising. So then there's the post-click experience from the ad to the landing page. One of the first things they like to see is does the landing page contain information about the product? You can't just have a VSL and no information on that page. You need to have an understanding and information about what is actually being sold. And then they'll go through with a similar lens as to whether or not the landing page makes any unsubstantiated claims. Does it withhold information? Employ clickbait techniques? And in particular, we see that when it comes to presale landers, they're really recommending people stay away from presale landers that do not add value. Now you were describing your presale lander, Sam, and it sounded like there was a lot of value there. But these are ones like you click on, what are five foods to avoid? That's what the ad is. You get to the presale and it doesn't tell you what the five foods to avoid are. It's just some more information. And then you have to click again to find out what the five foods are to avoid. So that whole process is very withholding, curiosity-based, and clickbaity. And Facebook is really cracking down on poor web experience. So you'll see sometimes people will get a poor ad experience and sometimes people will see a poor web experience. And that's when they have to click through multiple times to get the information that they're looking for. And lastly, the post-conversion experience. Facebook will send out now a feedback survey to people who have purchased off of an ad on Facebook. And they gather information and give a page score. And depending on that page score, they will automatically implement the penalty if you fall below a certain threshold. So I just wanted to mention that in case everyone wasn't aware of it. So we'll go through today and mostly focus on the first two. We'll look at the ads and then we'll look at the post-click experience.

## [15:07 - 18:07]

And, Stefan? On the post-conversion experience, that only applies to physical products, not to digital products. The way we phrase things like, you can't assume something about the viewer. In Facebook's mind, it makes the viewer feel uneasy and to feel that they're being targeted. The second thing we'll see is exaggerated claims, sensational language, shocking new research shows, we can cure cancer in five days.
